title: Detecting Connected Components in a Graph: Incorrect DFS

statement.markdown: You are given a graph represented as an adjacency list where each node is assigned a unique integer value. The graph consists of nodes connected by edges, and you need to detect all connected components within the graph using DFS. However, the provided code contains a subtle logical bug. Identify and fix the bug to correctly detect all connected components.
statement.inputFormat: The input format is a graph represented as an adjacency list where each node is assigned a unique integer value.
statement.outputFormat: Your task is to return the list of connected components, where each component is represented by a list of node values in ascending order.
statement.notes: You should only perform DFS on the nodes with unvisited neighbors.

examples.input:
Example input (matching the stdin format that main wrapper expects)
5
1 2
2 3
4
5

hints.text: Review the DFS traversal logic to identify the incorrect implementation.

editorial.title: Incorrect DFS Traversal Logic
editorial.intuition: The provided code incorrectly handles DFS traversal. Identify and fix the bug to correctly detect all connected components.
editorial.bruteForce: Brute force approach can be used to traverse the graph using DFS, but it is inefficient. Optimize the DFS traversal logic to correctly detect all connected components.
editorial.betterSolution: Use a more efficient approach to traverse the graph using DFS and correctly detect all connected components.
editorial.optimalSolution: Implement a correct DFS traversal logic that visits all nodes with unvisited neighbors to detect all connected components.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: Verify the correctness of the DFS traversal logic and the implementation of the solution.
editorial.dryRun: Test the solution with a sample input to verify its correctness.
commonMistakes: Failing to visit all nodes with unvisited neighbors
